description: A hard growth projecting from an animal's head is 'kurona', especially a true horn with a bony core and keratin covering. Ordinary speech can extend the word to an antler when structure is beside the point. A biological account can distinguish horns, antlers, and other head growths.
articulatory_notes: A closure at the soft palate releases 'k' into high-back rounded 'u'. A tap or brief trill leads into stressed rounded 'o'; dental nasal 'n' then opens into final 'a'.
usage_notes: A body part follows its animal in a noun phrase, as in 'nolika kurona' (the animal's horn). Shape, growth, display, contact, and danger remain separate descriptions.

semantic_domains.nature: a hard projecting growth from an animal's head
semantic_domains.physical: a durable pointed or branching body structure
